| **Date:** | 3/1/2026 |

**Investment**					                               
---

 

| Location | Seattle, WA |
| :---- | :---- |
| **One Line** | **AI-powered smart glasses for field workers and skilled technicians** |
| **Source** | **Introduced to Cassie/Jason by Mark Kosoglow** |
| **PVP Point** | **Jason Shuman** |
| **Round Size** | **$4-5m Pre-Seed** |
| **Valuation** | **\~$27m** |
| **PVP Investment \+ Ownership** | **\~$4m for \~15%** |

[**Deck**](https://docsend.com/view/2dvbmxux6dfgszx4) 

**What This Could Be**

*Plasma smart glasses have become the default medium of compute worn by every skilled tradesperson and technician in the US, auto-generating documentation and providing real-time work and sales coaching, turning all workers into elite performers. Plasma captures the full egocentric picture of what a technician sees, hears, says, and does, building a proprietary data flywheel that no one else can replicate. This data flywheel compounds over time as Plasma scales across auto service, HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and construction, becoming the process knowledge repository for the skilled trades economy: the platform that empowers real-time work assist for technicians across domains, that PE roll-ups use to rapidly onboard and upskill technicians, that OEM service networks rely on to enforce quality and compliance standards at scale, and that robotics companies will ultimately need to train and deploy in specialized vertical domains. In the skilled trades economy plagued by labor shortages and technician performance gaps, Plasma is positioned to become the defining AI infrastructure and operating layer for frontline work: the platform that no serious operator can afford to run their workforce without.*

**Executive Summary**  
Plasma is building AI-native smart glasses to automate documentation tasks and provide real-time work assist for skilled technicians across domains. Their initial wedge market is auto dealership service teams.

Today, auto service technicians lose hours per day to administrative tasks executed on mobile apps: documentation and reporting, taking photos and videos, communicating across teams, and following work instructions. These mobile apps, typically add-on features to decades-old legacy software systems, create friction for technicians who wear gloves, carry tools, operate machinery, have greasy or dirty hands, and work in dangerous, elevated, or confined spaces. As a result, dealership service teams are plagued by inefficiencies, consistently miss out on upsell opportunities, and regularly leak costs from denied warranty claims due to missing documentation. The same is true for almost any skilled technician domain.

Plasma is aiming to solve this with AI-native smart glasses that are hands-free and voice-driven, eliminating the need for a mobile app interface. Technicians can capture video documentation and narrate repair notes in real time instead of taking photos with a mobile app and manually typing in repair notes afterwards. Proposals for additional services can be quickly sent to waiting customers with photo evidence of what needs replacement/repair and an AI-polished summary of the technician’s voice over, driving increased upsell conversion. Photos are automatically captured during repairs/replacements for OEM warranty claims, preventing cost leakage from warranty chargebacks. Technicians are incentivized to adopt because they get paid by the repair and Plasma enables them to complete more jobs per day by automating documentation.

Plasma currently has 6 verbal commitments for design partnerships. They plan to complete 20 successful paid pilots by Q3 of this year.

The big vision for Plasma is to become the default compute surface for frontline workers the way that SaaS UI has for desk workers. Beyond auto dealerships (which we estimate as a $300M+ opportunity), Plasma plans to address the millions of US workers across other frontline and skilled technician domains: e.g. fleet and equipment service/repair, HVAC, plumbing, electric, and construction. **We estimate this to be a $5–10B+ total ARR opportunity** at modest ACVs, though we see a strong opportunity to capture more downstream workflows to expand ACVs and displace incumbents and AI-native startups. By accumulating a massive, proprietary, labeled egocentric video+audio dataset of frontline work, there is an additional 10x opportunity for Plasma to become the de facto robotics deployment and distribution layer for the trades, though we do not include this in our calculations.

Plasma was started by [Andrew Kinzer](https://www.linkedin.com/in/superkinz/), the former co-founder and CPO of [Outreach](https://www.outreach.io/), who we’ve been led to believe was one of the two most pivotal leaders behind Outreach’s success (next to the CEO). He is an exceptional product mind and a repeat founder.

He is raising a $4-5M pre-seed to make key hires in hardware, embedded systems, and engineering. Andrew is taking on Plasma as a solo founder.

**Investment Thesis**

* **Smart glasses are the most AI-native, efficient, and frictionless medium for frontline work and will become ubiquitous over the next decade.** Mobile apps (the status quo) face inherent form factor limitations (hands-on, no gloves, dirty fingers/screen, workflow interruption) and cannot rival the proactive automation and real-time efficiency-driving capabilities of smart glasses.  
* **Andrew is an exceptional and credentialed founder who we believe is uniquely positioned to architect the category-defining smart glasses product for the trades.** Andrew is a proven product leader who built a magical product at Outreach and helped the company to become the market leader despite late entry to the category. Creating the defining smart glasses company for frontline workers will require deeply thoughtful product nuance and orchestration, which we believe Andrew is uniquely capable of delivering. Although it was a failure, his last startup taught him the ropes around building a hardware business that was far more complex than what he is doing now.  
* **Smart glasses expand the fundamental limitation for technician-based services businesses: worker productivity.** Due to labor shortages, growing trades/services business is only possible through increasing productivity. These businesses are plagued by skill gaps and operational inconsistency across technicians. Smart glasses can level the playing field, enabling all technicians to operate at an elite level. We believe that in 5 years, no serious operator will be able to compete without leveraging smart glasses across their workforce.  
* **The dealership auto service market will serve as a high-velocity initial wedge.** Market feedback across numerous expert calls in auto service has validated that A) administrative burden and missed upsell opportunities are consistently felt pain points, and B) there is a clear willingness to adopt a smart glasses product that can deliver automation and drive ROI. Andrew has already secured 4 verbal commitments from auto dealership services departments. Additionally, multi-location franchise dealerships uniquely support short sales cycles (at individual locations) with fast group-wide rollouts (upon proven ROI).  
* **There is a clear market need across the trades (HVAC, plumbing, electric) that Plasma will be able to address in short order after auto service.** Just like in auto, trades technicians spend hours per day on paperwork, use decades-old mobile apps in the field, and frequently miss out on valuable upsell opportunities. Technician productivity is a key bottleneck and skill gaps limit operational consistency. We believe that AI-native smart glasses map perfectly to the trades and can address these pain points far more efficiently than mobile apps or audio alone.  
* **Capturing real-time annotated vision, voice, and audio data in the trades will create a compounding data flywheel that is difficult for incumbents or competitors to replicate.** Rich data with context on how physical work actually gets done will enable Plasma to unlock high-value downstream workflows unaddressable by competitors who lack data. This data makes the product increasingly valuable and defensible over time.  
* **Brand will be a strong moat in this market as Glasses are a wearable device that other trades will see in the market, which will lead to higher word-of-mouth marketing and adoption**  
* **The dramatic improvement in vision models, harnessed agents and battery technology lead to a strong why now for Plasma to build a JDCE and capture market share quickly**


**Gotta Believes**

* **Hands-free, AI-native egocentric glasses will become the dominant compute surface for frontline workers.** What is today done by typing into and clicking within software interfaces will be abstracted away and automated through glasses-enabled vision and voice capture. Technicians will embrace adoption because it helps them make more money and makes their job easier (automating boring documentation in the background, not adding another interface to learn).  
* **Auto service is a real wedge with enough unaddressed pain to achieve high-velocity growth.** Plasma's hands-free AI-native automation of technician inspection and documentation workflows must prove 10x better than mobile app UI-driven alternatives like XTime or DMS add-ons that live on iPhones, driving meaningful ROI for service centers through faster cycle times and increased upsell conversion rates.  
* **Starting initially in auto service will not limit Plasma’s success in home services, even though it delays their market entry**. This is a key GB since we view home services as a larger opportunity with more product overlap across sub-verticals and stronger organic growth vectors (e.g. many different services verticals interact on job sites). The market already has VC-backed competitors and moving quickly will be important in what could be a land grab dynamic.   
* **The data flywheel becomes a defensible, compounding moat over time.** Proprietary, labeled video+voice data capturing how physical work actually gets done will enable Plasma to unlock high-value downstream workflows unaddressable by competitors who lack data. This data must A) prove genuinely proprietary and hard to replicate, B) unlock meaningfully better product over time, and C) create high switching costs that are durable against well-resourced competitors/incumbents.  
* **Andrew is the rare founder who can credibly execute across software, hardware, multi-vertical GTM, and team building – as a solo founder.** He has genuine product DNA (Outreach), personal affinity for the trades (automotive rebuild, home remodel), 60+ customer discovery conversations before fundraising, and clear lessons from his previous hardware business. He understands critical GTM techniques that will be essential to growing quickly and has already scaled a category-defining business before.

**Why Now**

1. **Mobile apps have hit a productivity ceiling in the trades, while hands-free, voice-driven interfaces are now viable and affordable at scale.** The previous decade of touch-screen-first mobile interfaces have saturated their productivity and failed to remove friction inherent to their form factor (gloves, dirty hands, handling tools in confined/dangerous spaces, context switching). Smart glasses have crossed the threshold of viability and affordability to become the most intuitive and frictionless mode of compute for frontline workers.  
2. **A retiring workforce and deepening skill gap have made rapid onboarding and upskilling an existential priority for trades/technician businesses**. With millions of unfilled skilled trade jobs in the US, operators can no longer afford to wait years for green technicians to develop experience and intuition on the job. This creates urgent demand for technology that can A) compress the onboarding and upskilling of technicians, and B) systematize elite performance across entire workforces.   
3. **PE consolidation is creating a new class of sophisticated, profit-driven buyers hungry to deploy technology with real ROI.** Private equity is rapidly rolling up HVAC, plumbing, auto service, and electrical verticals, bringing with it the financial discipline and operational scale to buy and deploy AI productivity tools across hundreds of locations at once. This is a fundamentally different and more powerful buyer than the independent shop owner.  
4. **Rich, labeled, high-volume data is the bottleneck to realizing the latent potential of AI in the trades.** Foundation models are increasingly capable but lack the granular process data needed to deliver real value in the trades. The company that builds an always-on voice, vision, and audio capture layer will own the proprietary context that makes AI actionable in the trades.  
5. The intersection of cheap cameras, solid state batteries improving battery life, AI models like Gemini and harnessed agents make this possible today.

**Team**  
We believe that Andrew is an exceptional talent that is uniquely suited to lead a product platform shift. As a proven product leader who has built a category-defining business in a hyper-competitive market, we believe Andrew can assemble and lead an exceptional team and orchestrate a paradigm shift in how frontline workers do their jobs.

[**Andrew Kinzer**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/superkinz/) **(CEO, Founder):** Andrew was 1 of 4 original co-founders and CPO of [Outreach](https://www.outreach.io/), the sales engagement company that reached a $4.4B valuation at its peak. References on Andrew have described him as “a true engine behind Outreach’s success”, “one of the most thoughtful product people you will ever speak to”, and a “customer-obsessed, special product guy”. We have been led to believe that, next to Manny (the Outreach CEO), Andrew was the most pivotal to Outreach’s success and the key leader behind product vision and development.

After he stepped back from Outreach in March of 2020, he founded Tomorrow, a smart refrigerator consumer hardware product that dramatically extended the shelf-life of perishable produce. His rationale on starting the business was to tackle a hard problem outside of b2b software, build something in the physical world, and help address climate change by reducing food waste. Andrew was able to develop and validate proprietary technology capable of extending the shelf life of produce by 3-5x, but shut the company down after 4 years due to insufficient consumer demand.

His original inspiration for Plasma was realizing the capabilities of smart glasses could be extremely valuable to physical trades work. He then had 60 conversations across 14 role segments and 30 accounts, and eventually settled on dealership auto service due to 1\) clear pain points and need for the product, and 2\) fast, frictionless sales cycles.

Andrew is tackling Plasma as a solo founder. He was also solo on his last company and was clear that it was not an issue for him. He spent time searching for a Plasma co-founder but realized the best people he found looked more like “great early employees” as opposed to co-founders. As he got deeper into validating the market and closing design partnerships, it became clear he could “muscle it to here” solo and then use the extra equity to attract a very strong founding team. His first hires will be a Head of Hardware and an Embedded Software Lead (both already lined up) plus three full-stack engineers. 

**Market**   
Plasma’s initial wedge market is auto dealership service groups.

There are 18,000+ franchised dealerships in the US, with over 40% belonging to multi-location dealership groups: Plasma’s initial ICP. Group size ranges dramatically: most groups have 2-5 locations but many large regional brands have 10-50 rooftops and the largest dealership groups have hundreds of rooftops. 

The auto service function that Plasma sells into is highly consistent across group size. At the group level, the VP/Director of Fixed Operations oversees the service organizations across all locations and controls group-level buying decisions. At the dealership-level, auto service is run by the Service Manager, who Service Advisors (a customer-facing rep that acts as the ‘project manager’ on a repair job) and Technicians report up to. Across our diligence calls with dealership groups, the average dealership had \~20 bays, \~15 Technicians, and \~5 Service Advisors.

In addition to dealership auto service, we believe that Plasma’s auto product can address collision repair and auto body, fleet maintenance, and quick-service auto chains.

Beyond auto service and repair, Plasma will expand into home services and construction. We believe that home services represents a far larger market than auto with clearer product expansion opportunities to expand ACVs and stronger organic growth vectors (the trades are constantly overlapping at big job sites). HVAC, for example, shares many core criteria with auto service that make it a great fit for glasses (high paperwork burden per job, hands-on work environment, structured data needs, complex high-volume workflows) but also has high-value workflows like technician-led field sales that Plasma can address (e.g. real-time sales coaching, recommendations, and objection handling).

Over time, we believe that Plasma has clear expansion opportunities to A) go deeper into verticalized workflows, or B) expand across many other addressable skilled technician verticals.

*TAM*   
Overall, we believe that Plasma can address any skilled technician vertical where work is physical / hands-occupied and documentation burden meaningfully erodes productive or billable time.

We break down the market opportunity into 2 buckets.

The first bucket encompasses all auto, fleet, and heavy equipment repair. This includes all skilled technicians who service or repair vehicles, trucks, buses, and industrial/construction vehicles and mobile equipment. We estimate this as a $1.4–$4B ARR opportunity. Note: the initial wedge market of franchised dealerships represents a \~$300M+ ARR opportunity alone.  
![][image2]

The second bucket is home/commercial services. We made ACV assumptions for each vertical based on documentation-intensity and revenue generation opportunity, e.g. HVAC can absorb a higher ACV because it requires lots of documentation and sales recommendations/coaching can help technicians close more deals. We estimate this as a $3.1–$8.9B ARR opportunity. Note: we anchored to the $250-300 per rep per month ACVs of Rilla/Siro in home services, though we believe Plasma could deliver meaningfully more ROI.

![][image3]

Taking both buckets together, **we believe that the frontline workforce represents a \~$5–10B+ opportunity for AI-native smart glasses.**

*Competition*

* ***Direct VC-backed competitors building smart glasses for frontline workers*****:** We believe that Plasma will face the most heated early competition from VC-backed startups that are quickly moving to land grab in the trades and home services verticals. We believe that speed is important due to: 1\) the high switching costs associated with hardware, 2\) capturing an early lead to build a compounding data flywheel, and 3\) the strategic partnerships up for grabs with verticalized SoRs that may cement early winners (e.g. becoming the preferred partner in the ServiceTitan ecosystem creates an unfair distribution advantage). While speed is critical, we don’t believe the market is an all-out land grab (“first takes all”)  because 1\) glasses are relatively cheap (\~$150 ASP) and not “fixed” deployments like sensors or stationary cameras, and 2\) building the best-in-class user experience will require nuanced and opinionated software development and orchestration, i.e. this is not a commoditized product category where vendors are undifferentiated and interchangeable.   
  * [**Navigate**](https://www.navigate.ai/) **(construction restoration scoping, raised \~$20M by Elad Gil, Khosla, Fifth Wall):** founded by the former CEO and co-founder of Opendoor, [Eric Wu](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ericwu01/). Starting with a mobile app today and eventually moving to smart glasses, Navigate is selling into construction and commercial trades (HVAC, plumbing, roofing) to help automate inspections and job scoping. Today, a worker walks around a site, records video on their phone, and AI creates a structured scope of work with all identified line items. They are also advertising safety use cases (identifying violations in real time) and proactive work assist. Eric’s explicit vision is to equip field workers with smart glasses in order to instantly upskill them and boost productivity, while accumulating a moat of labeled egocentric data to eventually become the key construction robotics integrator.  
  * [**Robby**](https://www.tryrobby.com/) **(home services, backed by YC in W26 batch):** founded by two engineers and a former Bain consultant who focused on home services transformation. Current product is an AI-native mobile app for home services technicians to automate pre-job briefs, capture all notes and follow ups through voice AI, and provide real-time selling support through an AI chat interface to drive upsell. They claim $135K additional revenue per technician per year with 15% fewer call backs.   
* ***Incumbent vSaaS Systems of Record***: there is real risk of incumbent SoRs leveraging their commanding market share and installed base to quickly bundle and roll out smart glasses down the line. ServiceTitan has already mentioned it publicly. While Plasma aims to integrate and “play nice” with the SoRs instead of trying to replace them (similar to the Rilla/Siro approach), there is real risk if the incumbents want to own the glasses category: they own the customer, they have the scale and resources to bundle and undercut on price, and they can block third-party integrations (e.g. TrunkTools was removed from Procore API access). We are betting on the belief that Plasma will innovate far faster than incumbents and deliver a meaningfully better product experience. An embedded assumption to this belief is that a JDCE in smart glasses will require deeply thoughtful and nuanced software and model orchestration under the hood, which incumbents will struggle to replicate tactfully.  
  * **Dealerships and Auto:** CDK, Reynolds and Reynolds (together, CDK and R\&R represent \~70% dealership market share), [Tekion](https://tekion.com/) (founded 2016 pre-AI, but the most ‘modern’ DMS gaining market share), Dealertrack (Cox-owned)  
  * Trades / Field Services / Construction: ServiceTitan (clear majority market share, especially up-market), HouseCall Pro, Jobber  
* ***Adjacent vertical AI voice companies:*** there is a risk that adjacent AI-native voice companies will aim to compete in the smart glasses market over time.   
  * [Rilla](https://www.rilla.com/) and [Siro](https://www.siro.ai/) in home services: Rilla/Siro are voice AI sales coaching tools used by field sales and skilled technician teams in home services. They record sales conversations and provide sales coaching (“Gong for home services”). While they primarily target field sales teams (not technicians), some home services businesses double-leverage their technicians for sales and already use Rilla or Siro. In these instances, there is day-one product overlap between Plasma and Rilla/Siro in voice. Adding vision through smart glasses has clear strategic rationale for Rilla/Siro, allowing them to capture more workflows and deliver stronger ROI (automating photo documentation, voice-led equipment information lookup, real-time repair- and sales-coaching).  
  * [Mia](https://www.mia.inc/) and [Pam](https://www.pam.ai/) in dealerships and auto: while Mia and Pam are entirely focused on phone/reception and sales voice AI use cases today, it’s not implausible that they look to expand their product footprint to the “back of the house” in auto service to take on technician workflows with voice and vision via smart glasses. They are quickly expanding penetration in the dealership market and have pre-established trust and existing customer relationships. That said, we have zero information that leads us to believe smart glasses are actually on their radar.

**GTM**   
Plasma currently has 6 verbal agreements for design partnerships, 4 of which are with dealership auto service departments, Plasma’s initial wedge ICP. Andrew has landed these through cold outbound and in-person field sales (just showing up to dealerships). 

Auto dealership service groups are uniquely suited to short sales cycles and clear land-and-expand pathways. Initial sales have very short sales cycles and can be made at the dealership level (Andrew describes field sales as “walk up to the garage and pitch”). Land-and-expand can happen in two ways: 1\) dealership locations within a group occupy confined geographies and are not competitive with each other, so Service Managers meet regularly and share updates and best practices, which provides a clear path to organic growth, and 2\) the Director of Fixed Ops, who oversees all dealerships in the group, has the authority to push down software across multiple locations if ROI is already proven by one of the dealerships. 

After raising this round, Andrew will continue to lead sales himself as initial hires are focused on hardware and product development. He is targeting 20 successful paid pilots by the end of Q3 of this year.

**Product**   
Today, auto service technicians use mobile apps within or integrated to their Dealership Management System (e.g. CDK, Tekion, XTime) for each car that comes in for service. This includes Multi-Point Inspection (a video walkaround and routine diagnostic checklist), Additional Service Requests (a detailed estimate with pictures and rationale for each suggested repair/replacement) and Repair Order notes (“the three Cs” – complain, cause, correction). For warranty-covered jobs (30-35% of revenue), the tech must meticulously document the repair (pictures of the replaced part, exact serial numbers, etc.) for the OEM warranty claim. In total, techs spend \~12 minutes per job on paperwork plus a few more minutes taking photos and looking up information. Across a typical 8-car day, this means \~1.5 hours of admin work per technician. After a technician creates a report, it still needs to be polished and reviewed by customer-facing Service Advisors, who spend at least an hour per day reviewing customer repair packets.

These inefficiencies lead to 5 universally felt pain points in auto dealership service:

1. **Technicians spend \~1.5 hours per day on admin work** instead of executing on repairs.  
2. **Service Advisors spend 1-1.5 hours per day reviewing documents** instead of interfacing with customers.  
3. **Low media attach rates, low upsell.** When a dealership recommends additional repair services to a customer, it is best practice to attach a photo or video to show the customer the broken part that needs to be fixed/replaced. It is common knowledge that this drives upsell conversion and thus 90%+ “media attach rate” is a north star KPI across the industry. Across our conversations with dealership service teams, we saw an average media attach rate of \~70%, the lowest being 20%.   
4. **Slow customer packet delivery, less upsell.** When a customer is waiting in the shop while their car is being repaired, it is best practice to quote them on any additional service requests as soon as possible to optimize conversion. One operator we spoke with told us that when upsell recommendations are delivered within 15 minutes, upsell conversion is 45%+. For every incremental 15 minutes, conversion drops 10-20 points. Speed is paramount but bottlenecked by manual documentation and review.  
5. **Warranty chargebacks.** OEMs require meticulous documentation: before/after pictures of parts, part-level serial numbers, workshop manual procedure codes, etc. Warranty jobs make up 30-70% of revenue depending on the dealership but are frequently denied due to missing documentation (one dealership told us 5-7% of claims are denied). 

Plasma plans to address all of these pain points through a seamless AI-native product experience. With voice-driven smart glasses, techs can easily capture MPI and ASR photos and videos while narrating repair notes in real time. Plasma can then use this information to generate customer-ready quotes and repair notes. Pictures and videos for warranty claims are automatically captured and uploaded as well. Plasma can also enable shared vision with OEM tech support lines to improve repair troubleshooting.

This drives clear ROI: technicians complete more jobs per day and make more money (since they are paid per job), the dealership turns more volume per day, service advisors spend less time reviewing packets and more time closing upsell and building customer loyalty, media attach rates and speed to quote increase which drives upsell, and warranty chargebacks are reduced due to automated documentation capture.

In parallel, Plasma has collected labeled egocentric vision+audio data on how to diagnose and perform a specific repair. It knows the make, model, service history, how to diagnose the issue, how it was fixed, with what part, why it was fixed, how long it took, and how much it costs. The beauty is that the data is “self-labeled” in real time as the technician voices over the repair: e.g. “here you can see the brake pads are wearing thin, so I recommend replacing them, I am choosing this replacement part because it’s designated by the OEM for this make/model”. 

Over time, this allows Plasma to develop proactive work assist functionality to guide technicians on repairs in real-time. This would be a massive unlock that – in addition to allowing technicians to complete repairs faster with fewer mistakes – enables new or lower-skilled technicians to perform at master technician levels, driving elite-level, consistent operational performance across service teams.

![][image4]

Plasma will follow a phased product strategy across verticals with a deliberate wedge: start with high-frequency workflows that are low-complexity to automate (photo/video capture, dictated repair notes), collect data over time, then launch higher-value workflows (real-time repair assist), compound data, build more workflows, and repeat.

**Traction**  
Plasma has 6 verbal commitments for design partnerships: 4 dealership auto service departments (ranging from family-owned groups with 4-5 dealerships up to a large 50-dealership group), 1 construction GC, and 1 electrician firm. These design partnerships do not require contracts. Verbal agreements have been given and internal approvals are underway.

Plasma is targeting 20 successful paid pilots by the end of Q3 of this year. They will start with off-the-shelf glasses and in Q3 start rolling out prototype glasses for hardware validation. For the prototype glasses, they will partner with an ODM to launch ANSI-compliant lenses with industrial-grade frames.

Plasma plans to fully turn on monetization in mid-2027 once they have their own glasses in market (after prototyping, EVT/EVT, and certifications are complete).

We believe that Plasma can surpass $3M+ ARR in their first year in market. At a baseline price of $150 per month per technician, $3M ARR requires about 100 dealerships (\~15 techs per dealership on average). Given the short sales cycles and ability to sell across large dealership groups (50+ dealership groups are common), plus pre-proven ROI from paid pilots, we believe $3M is very reasonably achievable.

**Key Risks**

* Andrew, while he is a deeply experienced third-time founder and scaled Outreach to a multi-billion-dollar company, will be a solo founder building a complex hardware product aiming to sell into multiple different verticals. The product will require deep hardware, supply chain, embedded software, multi-modal AI, and data expertise. The GTM will require a nuanced understanding of different trades end markets and many integrations into verticalized software stacks.  
* We expect Plasma to face intense competition from fast-moving well-funded startups, particularly in home services and construction, in what may turn out to be a land grab market with strong first-mover advantages (hardware lock in, compounding data moat advantages, exclusive integration/distribution partnerships with incumbents). Speed-to-market and strategic vertical sequencing will be critical in shaping the category-defining company in smart glasses for frontline workers. Starting with auto service will delay Plasma’s entry to home services and construction, allowing first-movers to gain a further advantage and new entrants to further increase competition.  
* Incumbents with massive installed bases like CDK and Tekion (in auto) or ServiceTitan (in the trades) could quickly bundle and launch their own smart glasses, challenging Plasma’s standalone defensibility. AI-native players attacking adjacent workflows (like Rilla and Siro in the trades) could also launch their own glasses to capture vision in addition to voice.  
* Delivering on the promises of AI-native smartglasses (proactive voice and vision data capture for documentation, real-time work assist and sales coaching, voice data lookup, deep integrations into existing software across verticals) will be a significant technological feat. Expanding across industries will require verticalized workflow nuances and software integrations that don’t translate to other verticals.  
* The rapidly-evolving smart glasses market may create operational challenges: supply chain dependence, cross-OEM compatibility, installed base obsolescence. Swapping out hardware is more difficult than swapping out foundation model APIs.  
* Privacy concerns and end-user adoption may present real challenges. Recording technicians and customers with video and audio is unprecedented in many trades settings. Companies like Rilla and Siro (AI sales coaching for field service reps) have faced regulatory hurdles (varying state-by-state laws on single- or dual-party recording opt-in) and challenged end user adoption (sales reps are hesitant to allow their conversations to be recorded and scrutinized by management). 

Appendix

**Prototype Reference Design**

![][image5]

**Customer/ Expert Call Highlights** 

[**Daniel Neghala**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/daniel-a-negalha-b7a9a53b/) **\-- oversees Service Operations for McGovern Auto Group** 

* 40 dealerships across 24 OEMs, 590 total technicians, 21 lifts per rooftop  
* customer of [**XTime**](https://xtime.com/), a Cox-owned (acq. for $325M in 2014\) software that technicians use as a mobile app to take inspection photos/videos, write upsell reports and job notes, integrates nicely into the DMS. \-\> this is a direct competitor to Plasma in a different form factor. He gave it a 10/10 NPS  
* Techs are paid per job, incentivized to do more work  
* Techs spend \~12 minutes per job on inspection photos and admin work  
* **94% of job proposals include multi-media to drive upsell**  
  * it is well-known in the industry that this leads to higher upsell conversion, he incentivized it by offering a \~$10K holiday bonus to techs who added photo/video to 90%+ of their reports  
  * additionally, he said that **conversion rates are \>45% if the recommendations are sent to the customer within 15 minutes**, and conversion drops 10-15 points every 15 minutes.. so speed is a high priority  
* Service Advisors spend \<1 hour per day cleaning up technician notes before they go to the customer  
* Validated that documentation completeness is very important for getting OEM warranty reimbursements (30% of revenue)  
* Interestingly, he noted that some OEMs are selling subscriptions for AR glasses with pre-loaded work instructions specific to their vehicles (Ford, Subaru) \-- he really likes the idea and is paying for a few but doesn't know how much the techs use it  
  * could be an interesting expansion vector   
* Reaction to: Plasma and was very excited about it, even when I pushed back saying that his metrics seem strong already, but he sounded genuinely excited about the glasses for automation

[**Bejan Modarressi**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/bejan-modarressi-2aa094a6/)**, Body Shop Director at Koons Automotive**

* Largest publicly traded dealership group in US  
  * 190 dealers nationwide across all major brands (Ford, Toyota, Mercedes, etc.)  
  * all dealerships have service departments  
* techs are paid per job, incentivized to complete more (industry standard)  
* currently using Tekkion DMS, which has a mobile app add-on for techs to take inspection pictures/videos on their phones  
* techs spend 30 mins per job on paperwork  
* only 70% attach rate of multi-media for upsell, management wants it to be 100% because everyone knows it drives more upsell  
* Service Advisors spend at least 1 hour per day cleaning up technician notes before they go to the customer  
* validated that documentation is very important for getting reimbursements from OEMs on warranty work  
* sounded interested in Plasma if it works as advertised. Didn't sound 11/10 excited but said he would absolutely pilot it

[**Dwayne Kinsley**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dwayne-kinsley/)**, Corporate Fixed Operations Director at LA Car Guy**

* 12 dealerships across LA (Toyota, Lexus, Porsche, Audi, VW, Subaru, Honda, Kia, Hyundai, Genesis)  
  * range from 10 to 50-bay service centers at each dealership  
* Tech stack: CDK (main DMS), Karma (payments/communication), Call Review (analytics), Mia/Pam AI (inbound calls), recently moved away from XTime/DealerFX  
* **Techs spend \~1.5 hours of 8 hour shift on admin work**: inspection, MPI, and Additional Service Request (ASR) documentation  
* **Service Advisors spend 1-1.5 hours per day reviewing repair order packets** before they go to the customer (\~10 minutes per job)  
  * **Common to have 5-10 advisors each spending 1.5 hours per day doing this**  
  * "Automating customer packet review would allow me to eliminate Service Advisor headcount" \+ allow more customer-facing time from others  
* Warranty Claim losses  
  * **5-7% of claims are denied due to missing documentation** (OEMs require meticulous and specific photo and serial-level documentation)  
  * Warranty Pay can range from 30% (industry standard) to 60-70% depending on the dealership  
  * Major source of cost leakage for dealerships  
* **\~85% of MPI packets include multi-media but only \~60% of ASRs (additional service requests) include media**, but ASRs are where it matters the most.  
  * multi-point inspection video \= very high-level walkaround video  
  * ASR documentation is specifically showing a customer what is broken on their car... providing photos here is what really drives conversion  
* Very positive reaction to the Plasma pitch  
  * **"Sign me up\!"**  
  * Could eliminate 1-1.5 hours of admin work per tech per day, plus 1-1.5 hours of Service Advisor review (and lead to headcount reduction)  
  * Techs can make more money (paid flat per-job rate)  
  * Could improve warranty claim success rate with better documentation  
  * More documentation drives more upsell  
  * ***Additional suggestion*****: could enable remote OEM tech line support with shared vision (he sounded really excited about this)**

[**Joshua Utley**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/joshua-utley-023613189/)**, Service Manager at Earnhardt Auto Centers**

* 17 brands across 11 locations, founded 1951  
  * His store: 112 employees, 38 techs, 52 bays (16 across street expansion)  
  * 14 service advisors handling \~8 cars per day each  
  * techs paid flat per-job rate  
* Software stack: CDK (DMS), Auto Point (MPI), Text to Drive, Car Wars (call recording), Mia (voice AI receptionist)  
  * “5-8 different applications running at once” \- communication gets lost between systems  
* Admin work time investment per vehicle  
  * **Techs: 1.5 hours per day total** (8-12 minutes on paperwork/MPI per job)  
  * **Service Advisors: 1+ hour per day reviewing MPI and repair packets** across \~8 cars per day  
  * Total MPI Process: \~30 minutes per vehicle  
* **Only 20% multi-media attach rate** to customer packets (industry target is 90%+ since it drives higher conversion)  
* Missing documentation leads to warranty reimbursement denial, big problem  
* Very positive reaction to Plasma pitch:  
  * "we would definitely like to explore that"  
  * mentioned safety is a key aspect, "a great two-for-one" (safety and automation)... said it's very hard to get techs to wear glasses and he has to hound them all the time

[**Andreas Bouras**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/andreas-bouras-63943084/)**, Operations Manager at On Time Experts**

* $40M annual revenue HVAC/plumbing company in Dallas  
  * 80% HVAC ($32M), 20% plumbing ($8M)  
  * 50 HVAC technicians, 15 plumbers  
* Andreas Bouras: Operations Manager, reports to GM  
  * Oversees service managers, call center, warehouse  
  * Responsible for all software implementation and troubleshooting  
  * “The fixer” \- handles operational issues and efficiency  
* 4-tier technician system with different compensation models:  
  1. Green/maintenance techs: $15-25/hour training rate  
  2. Service/repair techs: Performance-based, 20% commission on repairs  
  3. Opportunity techs (10+ year equipment): 20% commission \+ manager coaching  
  4. Sales team: 10% commission on equipment sales  
* Maintenance program: $15/month subscription, $25 flat fee per system visit  
* 80/20 rule applies: 20% of staff (tiers 3-4) generate 80% of results  
* Goal: 3-4 calls per day, currently achieving 2-3  
* Core systems: ServiceTitan (CRM), XOI (photos/AI), Rilla (call recording)  
* Recent switches: Avoca → Broccoli (call center), due to reporting accuracy issues  
* Other tools: Podium, Lace, Dialpad, Samsara fleet management  
* XOI rated 8/10 \- strong for data capture and customer targeting  
* Rilla compliance challenge: Only 60-70% technician usage on good days  
  * “Forgot to turn it on” excuse common  
  * Needs forced compliance through workflow integration  
* Andreas reaction: “I would buy that yesterday” and “would even invest in that”  
* Key value propositions validated:  
  1. Pre-call briefing during drive time  
  2. Automated photo/video capture replacing manual iPad work  
  3. Real-time equipment information and troubleshooting support  
  4. Voice-to-text paperwork automation  
  5. Potential AI coaching during customer conversations  
* Current paperwork burden: \~40 minutes per job (20 min in-house, 20 min pre/post)  
* Critical success factors identified:  
  1. Must force compliance through workflow integration (like ServiceTitan)  
  2. Privacy concerns from homeowners need addressing  
  3. Technician adoption requires clear time-saving benefit  
* Texas legal advantage: One-party consent state, no homeowner notification required for recording
